comments.content:
Hi JMG,
I'm the one who asked about the Sphere of Protection last week.
I followed your advice and picked up the Regardie book on the Middle Pillar. I wanted to ask a couple of questions about it--
Regardie places the Tiphareth center at the solar plexus, rather than the heart. If you recall, you suggested using the Middle Pillar exercise to heal the apparent damage to my solar plexus center. So my questions are--
1. To do that, would you use the Regardie Middle Pillar structure, and place Tiphareth/Muner at the solar plexus, rather than the heart? Or, would you do use the heart center, but then, after the exercise, direct healing energy into the solar plexus (as Regardie suggests in the book)?
2. Why did you decide to use the heart rather than the solar plexus in the various versions of the Middle Pillar you published?

3. What would be the consequences of switching from the heart to the solar plexus or vice versa?

4. It's repeatedly happened, during the years that I've practiced magic, that I thought I was doing a ritual correctly, only to re-read the book in question (sometimes after years) and discover that I'd been doing it incorrectly the whole time. I am on vacation and don't have my books with me, but... I'm right that you locate the Tiphareth center at the heart, rather than the solar plexus, right?

5. While we're on the subject, do you know of a good general discussion of the body's energy centers?

Many thanks!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) You could do either one, but I'd recommend the second -- work with an undamaged center to start with, and then use the energy flow to heal the damaged center.

2) The version I originally learned, in W.E. Butler's The Magician: His Training and Work, used the heart rather than the solar plexus. I tried both and found the heart center gave better results for me. Later on, close study of Rosicrucian occultism (which does a lot of work with the heart center) convinced me that using the heart center was essential for avoiding certain personality imbalances that too often crop up among Golden Dawn practitioners.

3) Your best bet, once you've healed your solar plexus center, is try shifting it down for a while and see how it feels.

4) Yes, that's correct.

5) I wish there was one!

replies.content:

I am asking this question as I am doing the solar plexus centre when I do the middle pillar exercise. replies.title: Re: Middle Pillar
replies.content:
The Hermetic GD system has a tendency to excess Sun energy, which can lead to arrogance and egomania if not balanced out. Working with the solar plexus center can exacerbate that, since that center is associated with self-assertion. Working with the heart center instead tends to balance it out, since the heart center is associated with relationship with others and the various modes of love.

Whether or not you personally would benefit from using the heart center instead can only be determined by experience. I'd say give it a try and see how it feels.

replies.content:
That's very helpful, thank you.

Regarding the Middle Pillar-- I love this ritual. The only issue I've ever had with it though is that it seems to concentrate energy in the upper "dantien" (to borrow a term from somewhere else).

For much of my life I was very clumsy, despite being naturally athletic. The thing that finally helped me become more coordinated was practicing tai chi, which (as you know) teaches you to center your energy at the womb center and move from there. I realized after practicing tai chi for a while that I naturally concentrated most of my energy between my chest and my head, leaving my body to kind of follow after-- hence my lack of coordination. The Middle Pillar seems to, if nothing else, do nothing to address this imbalance.

At the same time, concerns about energetic contra-indications between Golden Dawn and Taoist practices led me to step back from tai chi... with the result that my clumsiness returned. (And this was the case even though I was spending upwards of 10 hours a week practicing external martial arts styles!) Since I've been on a break from Golden Dawn work, I've been practicing tai chi again, and finding it helpful in restoring my balance and coordination. It may be of interest to you to know that this has especially been the case since I started working with sword forms, particularly the dao (single-edged Chinese sabre.)

Anyway. I wondered if lowering the Tiphareth center in the middle pillar to the solar plexus might help with my general need to lower my center of gravity in order to move better. At the same time, though, I think I know which personality imbalances you're talking about... The solar plexus center as I understand it is associated with the will as well as the movement of subtle energy in and out of the system. I've personally know at least one yoga-person who has become an energetic vampire through, I believe, intense focus on the solar plexus. My guess is that the unbearable arrogance you see in a lot of GD'ers has its origin in this as well. So obviously, I'd like to avoid that.

I think I've begun to ramble and don't know if I have a specific question. Do you have any thoughts on how to reconcile the need to make use of the womb and solar plexus energy centers in daily life with the Middle Pillar? On that note, I don't suppose you've made any progress with the Golden Dawn-compatible tai chi-like practice you hinted at a few years back?

replies.title: (no subject)

comments.content:
Good evening JMG,

1. In your estimation, does TDS (Trump Derangement Syndrome for anyone reading this who doesn't know what this acronym means) likely have its own egregore? Do you think there are egregores that help fuel and/or magnify waves of mass hysteria and other pop culture fads? 2. On the topic of egregores, is it likely that certain gods of various cultures folklore and practices have been egregores or other types of thoughtforms which affect masses of people? The ancient Roman religion comes to mind with regard to this question, as the Romans had gods and goddesses for practically everything; seemingly for every little mundane this or that in everyday life, in addition of course to minor tutelary genii, nature spirits, ect. And then there was the“god” of a town or village, of a domus (notable family/house) or even the imagery of a cult devoted to a particular emperor.

3. Do you think that in the area of popular religion, the hypothetical future American culture might go in the direction of having a god/spirit for everything under the sun like what I was getting at above? You mentioned before that you don't think there will be any kind of organized pantheon. Does this mean a massively-disorganized (or simply, unordered) collection of gods? Or do you think this future culture might feature a decentralized smattering of differing weird cults? (Age of Aquarius after all, haha)

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) My take is that TDS doesn't have an egregor of its own -- it's the product of an existing egregor-system that's in the process of imploding messily. Chase TDS to its core and you find class bigotry -- Trump is the candidate of the deplorables, thus serves as the dumping ground for all the shadow projection the comfortable classes like to direct at their social inferiors. So the Bad Orange Man is the projected shadow of the comfortable classes: take what they say about him and turn it back on the people who are saying it, and you'll rarely be far from the mark. That's happening, in turn, because the managerial elite that took over the industrial world after the Second World War is completing the normal life cycle and losing its grip on power, and the egregor of their dominance is becoming unstable in the usual way prior to going down like the Hindenburg.

2) Every god is a combination of a cosmic force and an egregor co-created by that force and a human community, working together, to allow interaction. The Lares and Penates of an ancient Roman house, for example, were egregors linked with certain divine forces; they expressed only a very narrow, specific aspect of those forces, but they did so in a way that brought Roman domestic life into harmony with the cosmic pattern. Most polytheist societies end up doing this. Every traditional East Asian home, for example, has a shrine to the kitchen god -- Zao Jun in China, Jowangsin in Korea, Sanbo-Kojin in Japan, and so on -- who reports on the family's doings to the divine court in heaven each year; that serves the same function.

3) My guess is that the future American culture will be more likely to focus on individual guardian spirits, but there will also likely be plenty of broader deities and weird cults -- that's certainly something that featured here in Native American times, with medicine societies and the like as a very common feature of religious life.

comments.title: Banishing ritual advice
comments.content:
Hello, JMG- my apologies for writing at such length, but I have a question that requires a bit of background:- I’m just finishing up my work in the LRM book (on the final lesson), and have been going at a slowish pace (started last February), so I’ve been practicing the Lesser Banishing Ritual of the Pentagram daily for almost a year now. As a bit of background, I’m a practicing Christian, baptized Catholic but mostly attending liturgy at an Orthdox church for the last several years. Last week, somewhat on a whim I tried performing the “Christian banishing ritual” you’ve posted a few versions of, and was surprised at the strength of the effects- immediately upon performance, both my own mind and the space where I was practicing felt noticeably different, for lack of a better word, brighter and cleaner. Though I wouldn’t say I’ve gotten no results from the LBRP, I’ve never felt so pronounced an energetic effect on the space around me- i got a pretty strong sense that “this is what banishing is supposed to feel like!”. I’ve been performing this every morning for a week now, and results have been consistent. I can think of a few reasons why this might be the case- I’m pretty terrible at visualization, and when doing the LBRP, I’m consistently plagued with doubts that the efficacy of the ritual is in some way dependent on the quality of my visualization and vibration, and that I’m therefore doing a subpar job, to subpar effects. The “Christian banishing”, by contrast, felt rather out of my hands, dependent instead on the grace of a being (Christ) I had a relationship with and trusted- it was easier to have faith that it would “work”, as opposed to depending on my own meager abilities. BUT- and here’s where the question comes in- I feel I’ve also learned a lot from my daily LBRP practice- it’s slowly helped me improve my visualizations, for example, and given me a better sense of working with elemental energies and relating to the archangels invoked- it feels valuable in all sorts of ways. Additionally, I hope to continue studying the Golden Dawn tradition, and work my way through “Paths of Wisdom”, and it feels like the LBRP is pretty baked into the system, with a number of things built on it as a foundation, so it’s not something I just want to discard. For the past week, my practice has been to do both rituals daily: the “Christian banishing “ one first thing in the morning, combined with morning prayers and reading and briefly meditating upon the daily gospel reading from the orthodox lectionary, and then, the LBRP and Middle Pillar Ritual followed by discursive meditation on topics from the LRM coursework in the evening just before dinner. I guess my question is if this seems workable to you, or if it’s too mix-and-match, beginner trying to pile on too many potentially incompatible practices, and, if the latter, what you’d advise in my situation?

Thank you!
-NPM

replies.title: Re: Banishing ritual advice
replies.content: That practice -- Christian ritual, prayer, and Bible study in the morning, and Hermetic ritual and meditation in the evening -- is what several Christian occultists of my acquaintance practice, and they have excellent results with it. I see no reason why you can't do the same thing, and so long as you're willing to invest the time and effort, your results will likely be as good as theirs.

replies.title: Re: Banishing ritual advice
replies.content: Same thing with practicing the Christian banishing in the morning, SoP and meditation in the evening? No interferences?

replies.title: Re: Banishing ritual advice
replies.content: Works either way. The Hermetic GD system was designed to be compatible with Christian religious practice -- many of the order's members were Anglicans, after all.

replies.title: Re: Banishing ritual advice
replies.content: JMG I am working through Gareth Knights 'Experience of the inner worlds.'  When I am done with that would moving on to your learning Ritual magic be a good idea?  Would they fit together well?

replies.title: Re: Banishing ritual advice
replies.content: It would be a change of system, but yes, that would be a workable next step if you want to go on to study ceremonial magic.

replies.title: Re: Banishing ritual advice
replies.content:
For what it's worth, I had the same trouble with the LBRP seeming less effective than it could be because I subconsciously felt I had to do it "perfectly" when I first started.

On the occasion that I would randomly try other banishings (like one from a Norse book), I had a much more relaxed mindset because I didn't care as much about the result. Paradoxically, that made the ritual feel more successful, probably because I wasn't programming to do poorly myself with a negative thought virus.

This need for perfection in the LBRP gradually went away as I got used to the natural fluctuations in practice (some days the visualizations seem more intense, etc). And going into the ritual with the goal of just keeping the general intent in mind during the visualizations, and accepting imperfections, helped a lot for me. Even if my visualization is worse one day, I know I'm formulating the right intent as best I can that day.

comments.title: Cabala, Greek Thought
comments.content:
On the topic of the Tree of Life-- I've been reading through Neoplatonic thinkers lately, struggling to make sense of them. The thing that keeps occurring to me, especially when I read Proclus, is that the Tree of Life is a straightforward visual diagram of Neoplatonic thought.

Proclus talks, for example, about how the power of any higher cause endures beyond that of lower causes. Thus Soul is present to living things. Intellect, though, is higher than Soul, and so there are more things that have Form but are not ensouled than there are living things. Finally, mere unformed matter lacks either Form or Soul, but it does have existence-- thus, the One is present to it. The One is primary, and so its power endures beyond secondary and tertiary powers, down to the very last thing.

Reading this, I realized that another way to say it is "Kether is in Malkuth, and Malkuth is in Kether."

replies.title: Re: Cabala, Greek Thought
replies.content: I'm convinced, and believe I will be able to prove in a future book, that the Cabala originated in Middle Platonism, among that end of the Platonist scene that was strongly influenced by the Pythagorean revival. The Tree of Life itself is adapted from a Pythagorean diagram -- the geometrical tetraktys, composed of a point, a line, a triangle, and a tetrahedron.  (Draw them in that order from top to bottom sometime, with the triangle and tetrahedron pointing down.) You can find a remarkable number of basic Cabalistic concepts in the writings of Middle Platonists such as Plutarch. Thus it's not surprising that Neoplatonism, which drew so heavily both from Middle Platonism and from Neopythagoreanism, would be so completely compatible with it!

replies.title: Re: Cabala, Greek Thought
replies.content:
Maybe the drawing would be like this one:
https://books.google.com/books?id=T7pHLvaYGfAC&pg=PA102

replies.title: Re: Cabala, Greek Thought
replies.content: Why, yes, it would.  ;-)

replies.title: Re: Cabala, Greek Thought
replies.content:
It is plausible that Cabala's primary influences came from Greek sources, but it also contains some deformations coming from Judeo-Christian theology. For example, the path of attainment in Cabalistic Tree of Life is only upward, which implies that material world is assumed to be inherently "inferior" or "source of evil". However, Neoplatonists and most of the other Greek schools didn't despise the material world or their physical bodies (for example Plotinus had polemics against Gnostics on this issue).
Also, Cabalistic Tree of Life falls short in incorporating cyclical phenomena (like ekpyrosis, seasonal cycles, involution & evolution, birth-growth-death-rebirth cycles). I'm not an expert on initiatory maps, but I think a good initiatory map should incorporate a cyclic path (or a spiral path in 3D) which correlates well with the actual processes that happen in the macrocosm. Just like every being in the universe revisits natural cycles in their proper times (either the exact cycle or an analogous cycle at a higher level), once in a while an initiate must also revisit the most elementary teachings and experiences of his/her training (regardless of his/her degree of attainment), in order to strengthen his/her background and to avoid ego-inflation. I think that kind of path would satisfy the true meaning of the word "initiation" better. For my taste, the Wheel of Life in Druidry provides a better approximation of reality. I don't mean to offend Cabalists, I have just intended to argue its potential drawbacks.

replies.title: Re: Cabala, Greek Thought
replies.content: The distaste for matter and the lack of cyclical processes are found in a lot of presentations of Cabala, but they're not inherent in the tradition itself. If you read Jewish Cabalistic literature, the notion that the material world is evil is rejected in no uncertain terms, and a great deal of lore on cosmic cycles is included -- the Hebrew term for such cycles is shemittah. As with so many other rich spiritual traditions, the difficulty comes because too many modern students don't take the time to familiarize themselves with the deeper levels of teaching before rushing something into print, and replace the challenging insights of the original tradition with half-digested notions taken from current pop culture.

comments.title: language learning resource
comments.content:
Hi JMG and all,

Please delete if you feel this is not appropriate, but I thought I'd share a recent disocevery that is challenging and delighting me lately. I decided to learn a new language to challenge my brain to work in a different way and experience how that alters my perceptions of the world and enriches my magical practice. There are 35 language options available at present, including Latin and modern Scottish Gaelic, modern Irish, and modern Welsh.

For those striving for fluency and advanced work in another language Duolingo won't take you that far. But it's nice friendly place to get a start. I'm not in it for the points and competitions, but if you're into that, there's opportunity to compete/connect/mark progress with others.

It's beeen so much fun, and so refreshing for me lately I just had to share.

Diolch,
Bonnie

comments.title: Christian Banishing Ritual
comments.content:
I recently took up the Christian Banishing Ritual designed here over the last couple months, largely because it seemed very intuitive—I had it memorised practically the first time I read it and while I do intend to finish mastering the SoP, it’s taking me ages to progress in that and the time it’s been taking me in the morning to do is causing me to shirk actually doing it, and the conditions of my immediate surroundings demand a banishing ritual that I can make sure to fit in every day without excuses.

That being said, there was one aspect of it that worried me, in that I recall you saying before with regards to the LBRP that practicing it by itself for an extend period of time could be unwholesome, as you’d effectively start banishing your own life force, and so it needed to be balanced by the invocational power of the Middle Pillar; further, that this was one of the plus sides of the SoP, in that it combined banishing and invoking into one rite (an effect I definitely felt). I’ve not been doing the CBR (it does seem to want a more elegant name, that makes it sound like it’s meant for banishing Christians!) for long enough to tell, but the rite as written seems to be similar to LBRP in lacking an invocatory element, and I wonder if that might constitute the same weakness of hyper-sterility (if you will) that you noted before.

A potential solution did occur to me, but I wanted to consult your expert opinion before attempting it. The following is the collect for the Devotional Prayers at Close of Day from the Episcopal Book of Common Prayer, and seems to combine both invocation of a blessing and a banishment of harmful forces. I wonder if it, or some variation thereof, would be workable in the quarters in place of “In the name that is above all other names etc”...or if I’m just overthinking this (or missing something) and it’s not actually a problem in the first place?

“Visit this place, O Lord, and drive far from it all snares of the enemy; let your holy angels dwell with us to preserve us in peace; and let your blessing be upon us always; through Jesus Christ our Lord. Amen.”


EDIT: Aaaand, a bit of hunting through earlier less-than-careful reading turned up where you already answered this question to someone else – “Yes, but not necessarily a ritual of the same kind. What I'd advise, since I gather you're Catholic, is to pray the Rosary every day. That's a potent invocation, and will balance the masculine Christ current of the ritual with the feminine Mary current.” I’m not Catholic, rather an Episcopalian/Anglican Druid, but a lot of the older Catholic practices appeal very strongly to me and I’ve been meaning to pick up praying the Rosary for a while now. I recently saw Agostino Taumaturgo describe the Rosary as a “complete magical system unto itself”, and my first thought was “that’s awesome, but...doesn’t it lack a banishing ritual?” Looks like the two were meant for each other!

replies.title: Re: Christian Banishing Ritual

It's got 33 beads and is used with a wide range of prayers in the Anglican tradition; the link I've just given includes some of them. Might be worth considering...

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Dear JMG,

1. On this week's post on the main blog, you responded to a commenter about his concerns he is spending his life stuck in a job to make money, you suggested that he might regret spending his time this way when he was on his deathbed. I am in a similar boat, in that my life orbits my well-paying but mentally demanding salary class job. I've been meditating a lot on the concept of intentionality which you introduced in the DMH, and on trying to focus on what one has, rather than what one wants. I'm struggling to resolve a binary I've set up: if I should focus on what I have, then I should appreciate my job for what it is, appreciate my good fortune in being able to exist without the struggle for money or with working multiple stressful and low-paying jobs that many others have to do, that I'm wealthy compared to those in the third world, etc.

So this implies I should pay attention to these positive aspects rather than focusing on what I want: more free time to exercise, to contribute somehow to the wider community, to be outside more, to learn to garden and play music properly, and so on. Because according to the concept of intentionality in the DMH, if I focus too much on wanting these things, then they move further away. How do I move towards these things without focusing on the lack of them?

2. The only answer I've got so far is that the problem is the focusing: the wanting and the having should balance each other, and focused on as such. Earth, the element of manifestation, would correspond to having, and Fire, the element of will and desire, corresponds to wanting, and the two are in opposite directions in the DMH, and thus keep the other in check.

3. Maybe slightly related to the first question: what's your opinion on the notion of the Good Life or Eudaimonia? I understand that it has to do with living a virtuous life, but what virtue means in this case has changed through time. I like the definition I found by Zeno, who said it was a life lived in accordance with nature. I wonder if it relates to one of the goals of magic that I've seen you note: to become most completely oneself.

Thanks again!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1 and 2) There's a difference between wanting something and willing something. If you decide to get a different job so you have more free time, then make it happen -- don't sit there wanting it. Take action with your whole self, make the changes you need to make, and accept the consequences wholeheartedly. Mages don't want, they will -- and so they accomplish. You can do the same thing.

3) My take is that what counts as the good life differs for every single being in all of existence. One size emphatically does not fit all; the question "what is the best way to live?" is one that confronts every one of us at every moment, and it has no one right answer.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) So if I read this correctly, both will and want come from the same place, a desire for something. The difference is that wanting remains wanting, a focusing on the desire for something without action toward it, whereas will is the conscious choice to realize a desire by moving towards attaining it. Part of willing something comes from first recognizing if one already has aspects of what one desires, and focusing on that first.

As an example of the difference, I thought of this: I remember a colleague at work who kept complaining that he wasn't being given interesting work, so he didn't apply himself, doing the most minimum possible because he wasn't being given interesting projects, and therefore never getting any interesting projects, whereas another concentrated on the projects he had, even though they weren't interesting, and was rewarded with increasingly interesting projects. Would that be more in line with what you mean?

replies.title: Re: Magical geography

Tripp,
I've worked in Evansville for the past three years as part of a territory that extended up north about a third of the state to Bloomington, IN, where I've lived for a long time. Evansville is unique in Indiana because it has been isolated for so long by the underdeveloped highway system that served that corner of the state. Culturally and economically it has been more a part of Kentucky, Illinois and Missouri. That is changing due to a new interstate connecting it to the "yankee" north through Indianapolis. It is deep into an economic boom that may be reaching a plateau.
Business there has largely been conducted on a southern patronage model, but again that is rapidly changing due to the new connectivity.
Trades are still controlled by unions so wages tend to be higher for the skilled trades, which in turn helps other wages. A couple of universities and a small symphony help to elevate the cultural offerings in an otherwise typical old river town where traditional christian values are the norm. The fairly recent casinos stand out in contrast to the traditional culture of Evansville.
The land in general has been under the plow for a long, long time and seems tired. There are some Indian mounds not to far away in Illinois. Most of the hardwood wilderness will be found south in Kentucky or east a couple of hours in Hoosier National Forest. Some of the best peaches and melons grown anywhere are just north of the area.
Hope this helps some.
Gawain

comments.title: Ego
comments.content:
Hi JMG! Thanks for hosting this Q&A! I’ve learned a lot from reading the answers and discussions here over the past several years.
For a while now, I’ve been doing a daily routine of SOP, discursive meditation, journaling, and I Ching, in that order. One of the things that the Sage emphasizes is overcoming your Ego, and developing your Superior, or Higher, Self. One of the conclusions that I’ve come through in my meditations is that if we were to put the Sage’s teachings about the Ego into a Druidical context, than the Ego is all of instincts, aspects, and learned traits that are left over from previous incarnations as an animal. The Superior Self are the parts of you that need to be developed in order to allow you to ascend into the next plane of existence. Thus, the conflict between the Ego and the Superior Self is one of the main interior conflicts which every human experiences. One of the ways that I have experienced this conflict is how all of the the things that my Higher Self says that I should do, (which are things that I actually suspect would make me happy) invariably get overridden my by Ego giving me a list of things that I “should” do, presented in a way to make them seem necessary to my survival. Is there anything that I can do, aside from the aforementioned daily routine of prayer, meditation, and divination, to help resolve this conflict in favor of my Higher Self?

replies.title: Re: Ego
replies.content:
What you're asking is perhaps the most important issue that every student of every system of spiritual teaching has to contend with. Your conscious self is always poised between its past and its future -- between the habits and conditions that make up what you're calling the ego, and the potentials for wisdom and grace that make up the higher self. What you need is the will and wisdom to choose the way of the higher self -- and getting those qualities is the whole point of spiritual practice. When you've achieved that, you won't be a student any more, you'll be a master.

And yes, you get there through the labor of your spiritual practices, and the subtler but equally important labor of telling your ego to take a long walk off a short pier. ;-)

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

Even though I admire the knowledge of the ancient polytheistic adepts (like Egyptian Hierophants, Hindu Brahmins, Roman Neoplatonists, Celtic Druids, Persian Magi etc.), there is still a common point among them that disturbs me: all these adepts were in a privileged position in their societies, and they were using their knowledge for the benefit of their ruling classes. So, can we deduce that the downfall of ancient polytheistic clergy was bound up with the accumulated karma of misusing their knowledge at the expense of the lower classes of their societies?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I'm going to start by challenging the root assumption that underlies your question. Human beings, like all other social primates, are innately hierarchical; all human societies dispense privilege unequally -- even among hunter-gatherer peoples, there are some who have more authority and more say in collective decisions than others. Show me a group that claims to be non-hierarchical and I'll show you a group that replaced an overt hierarchy with a covert one which, being covert, is less subject to checks and balances and more likely to permit abuses of power.

That being the case, the ancient polytheistic adepts -- like their medieval and early modern monotheist equivalents -- dealt with that reality by pursuing whatever degree of privilege would enable them to pursue their work, and by acting as advisers to the ruling classes in order to inject some common sense and some ethics into the minds of the rulers. You mention the Druids; sometime go look up the Audacht Morainn, an ancient Irish document that embodies Druid teachings for kings. It's all about how a king needs to live by the fir flathemon, "the truth of sovereignty," the rule of absolute honesty, justice, and honor that kings must abide by if they and their kingdoms are to thrive.

It's a modern superstition that every privileged person necessarily abuses their privilege at the expense of the less privileged. Does that happen? Sure, but it also happens quite often that people with privilege recognize that their privileges imply heightened responsibility toward the less privileged. (The phrase to look up here is noblesse oblige.) I'd encourage you to turn a more skeptical eye toward modern prejudices about privilege, and do some reading in the works of the ancient polytheistic adepts you mention; you may be surprised what you find.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Do you think that idea everyone with privilege always abuses it exists to justify the way our privilged classes abuse their privilege?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Partly. Nietzsche also suggested that the claim that everyone with privilege always abuses it is a nice way to dress up bitter jealousy and hatred as morality.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I'm not sure what he meant by that. Do you know where he discussed it so I could take a look?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: It's in The Genealogy of Morals. He's arguing that people who are filled with hatred and jealousy for the more successful like to pretend that no, they're not filled with hatred and jealousy, they're bursting with moral indignation because of the awful way the privileged treat everyone else -- they're the good people, you see, and the privileged are the bad people! If this sounds familiar, hey, that's not Nietzsche's fault...

comments.title: Pantheistic Drudiry
comments.content: Which branches or paths of druidry take a pantheistic view of nature/the universe?  Also, are there any that describe nature as constantly unfolding (process metaphysics), and work magic based on that?

replies.title: Re: Pantheistic Drudiry
replies.content: Druidry isn't really about "branches" or "paths." Most Druid orders have a free mix of people with very diverse ideas about nature, the universe, and the gods. There are lots of Druid patheists -- did you know that the word "pantheism" was invented by John Toland, an influential figure in the early days of the Druid Revival? It wouldn't surprise me at all if there are Druids out there who've taken up Whitehead's process metaphysics and worked up practices on that basis -- it's the kind of thing that would appeal to people in the Druid community -- but I don't happen to know any personally. Fortunately, you don't have to wait for someone else to do that; pick up an introductory book on Druidry (yes, I'll recommend my book The Druidry Handbook), explore the symbolism and practices, and see if you can work out how to relate them to a pantheist process spirituality. There's enough flexibility in the traditions that I doubt you'll have much trouble.

comments.title: Planetary magic when a planet is in its detriment
comments.content:
When a planet is in it's detriment or fall, are its energies just weakened, or will magic done with that planet be completely unsuccessful or even backfire?

For example, the reader last week who you recommended should do hexagram rituals and invoke Raphael to help with legal trouble; would Mercury being in Pisces negate the effects, or just make them weaker? I'm considering a similar ritual with Mercury, hence the question.

Thanks!

replies.title: Re: Planetary magic when a planet is in its detriment
replies.content: It really depends on the type of magic. Golden Dawn-style magic is relatively resistant to planetary effects, since it draws its energy from the spheres of the Tree of Life rather than  the planets as such. A lot also depends on your natal chart -- if your natal Mercury is afflicted, you need to be more careful than if it's strong. Classic astrological magic, by contrast, should never be done when the planet you want to invoke is afflicted -- it can backfire in a very big way.

comments.content:
Hi JMG,

My grandmother was recently diagnosed with late stage cancer and doesn't have long to live. Her doctor has told her that because of the position of the tumor, in close proximity to major nerves, she will eventually end up in overwhelming pain. The doctor told her that in our state, death with dignity is a legal option. She is considering it and wants to speak to her pastor to get his opinion, which I encouraged her to do (speak with the pastor, that is).

I know that from an occult perspective, suicide (which the death with dignity option is) can cause you issues after death. From the perspective of being very near death already, what would the implications of suicide be?

Thank you

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
If death is already imminent, it's not much of an issue; she'll just have to hang around this plane a few days longer, which can happen anyway and will give her time to visit the people she cares about (and some of them might see her -- appearances of ghosts right around the time of death are the most common of all ghostly phenomena). This is also something where prayer can help a great deal -- while she lives, she can make connection with her deity, and when she's gone, you can help her by praying for her.

In her position, for what it's worth, I'd probably choose suicide and put up with the minor inconveniences.

replies.title: Re: Sex Drive

replies.title: Re: Sex Drive
replies.content: Young man here: so many guys, especially under the age of 40 or so, are horribly addicted to porn. This has a number of effects: the first is that a sizable number of men (my guess is around 10-15%) have dropped out of the dating/sex game altogether. These guys are so bad that if a girl asks them to have sex, they're not interested: they'd rather spend the time watching porn. Then a sizable number of men are interested but unable to perform; many of whom also have stopped trying to find dates, and who will politely decline any offer of sex.  So it doesn't surprise me in the least that there are young women out there who are unable to find partners.
